event_blocks.summary: In a warmly lit room, a man in a striped shirt, glasses, and goatee introduces the historical significance of food to Native identity, transitioning to an animated depiction of a 14-year-old Billy Frank Jr. in a red coat being arrested by police officers near a river at night in December 1945, his defiant cry of 'LEAVE ME ALONE! I LIVE!' echoing as he is taken to a police car, followed by a mugshot-style illustration. The speaker, identified with the "Crash Course Native American History" title card and upbeat intro music, explains this was an early Fish Wars battle, showing historical photos of Billy Frank Jr. fishing in 1973 and noting his numerous subsequent arrests. The narrative shifts to historical black-and-white photographs of Nisqually fisherman Johnny Bobb and Tribal Member Lou Ann Squally handling fish, emphasizing the Nisqually people's deep connection to salmon as gifts to be honored, even at the cost of jail time, as evidenced by grainy images of arrests during the Fish Wars (1968–1970). The speaker then defines 'Sovereignty' as 'The right of Native nations to govern themselves' and 'Food Sovereignty' as 'The right of Native nations to use the foods that have sustained their communities since time immemorial,' illustrated by a basket of traditional Indigenous foods, dried corn cobs, and footage of Suquamish salmon preparation (2018). The segment concludes with an illustrated June calendar depicting strawberry plants, referencing the Algonquian 'strawberry moon,' and an animation of a diverse protest group holding signs like 'THIS LAND IS OUR LAND' and 'HONOR THE TREATIES,' inspired by Billy Frank Jr.'s resistance.

Figure in red coat.description: A hand-drawn character with dark hair, wearing a red coat, identified as Billy Frank Jr. The audio reveals he was 14 years old in 1945 when he was arrested on the Nisqually River for checking fishing nets, shouting 'LEAVE ME ALONE! I LIVE!' He is depicted surrendering, being escorted by officers, in a mugshot, fishing, and later inspiring non-violent resistance. The audio notes he was a member of the Nisqually Nation and would be arrested over 50 more times.

Man in striped shirt.description: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a room with framed artwork and shelves in the background, speaking directly to the camera about how food defines Native people, and later explaining the concepts of 'Sovereignty' and 'Food Sovereignty'.

Police officers.description: Silhouetted and later blue-uniformed figures who arrest Billy Frank Jr. in 1945, with one officer heard saying, 'You're under arrest!' They are depicted escorting him and later flanking him in subsequent arrests.

Traditional Indigenous foods basket.description: A close-up of hands holding a woven basket filled with colorful corn, herbs, and other traditional Indigenous foods, with a turquoise beaded necklace visible on the person’s chest, shown as the speaker describes the distinct, culturally defined relationships Native communities had with their traditional foods.

Arrests during the Fish Wars photograph.description: A grainy, colorized historical image showing law enforcement officers arresting individuals during the Fish Wars in Washington (1968–1970), framed with colorful geometric borders, visually illustrating the speaker's point about 'facing jail time' for protecting fish.

Historical photograph of Johnny Bobb.description: A black-and-white photo of a man holding a large fish, captioned 'Nisqually fisherman Johnny Bobb', framed with colorful geometric borders, shown as the speaker discusses the Nisqually people's connection to fish.

Suquamish salmon preparation footage.description: A video clip showing salmon being smoked over a fire using a traditional wooden rack, captioned 'Suquamish salmon preparation Washington (2018)', framed with colorful geometric borders, shown as the speaker describes the distinct, culturally defined relationships Native communities had with their traditional foods.

Sovereignty definition graphic.description: A split-screen graphic with the Man in striped shirt on the left and a text box on the right defining 'Sovereignty' as 'The right of Native nations to govern themselves', set against a green textured background with colorful borders, with the definition being spoken aloud by the Man in striped shirt.

Food Sovereignty definition graphic.description: A split-screen graphic with the Man in striped shirt on the left and a text box on the right defining 'Food Sovereignty' as 'The right of Native nations to use the foods that have sustained their communities since time immemorial', set against a green textured background with colorful borders, with the definition being spoken aloud by the Man in striped shirt.

Historical photograph of Lou Ann Squally.description: A black-and-white photo of a woman crouching in shallow water handling a fish, captioned 'Nisqually Tribal Member Lou Ann Squally', framed with colorful geometric borders, shown as the speaker discusses the Nisqually people's connection to fish.

Dried corn cobs display.description: A wooden surface displaying rows of dried corn cobs with multicolored kernels, alongside jars of beans and other grains, shown as the speaker describes the distinct, culturally defined relationships Native communities had with their traditional foods.

June calendar illustration.description: An illustrated calendar page for June, showing strawberry plants with green leaves, white flowers, and red strawberries, under a red sun and a blue sky with stars, drawn on lined paper, aligning with the speaker's mention of Algonquian tribes knowing June as the 'strawberry moon'.

raw_segments: The segment opens with Jay Gim, a man in a blue and brown striped shirt, glasses, and a goatee, seated in a warmly lit room. He begins narrating a historical event from a dark winter night in 1945, as the scene transitions to a hand-drawn animation. We see a figure, identified as 14-year-old Billy Frank Jr., in a red coat by a nighttime riverbank, illuminated by flashlights from two silhouetted police officers. An officer's voice declares, 'You're under arrest!' to which Billy Frank Jr. animatedly responds, 'LEAVE ME ALONE! I LIVE!' raising his hands in surrender. The officers then escort him towards a police car, followed by a mugshot-style illustration of him holding a sign dated 'DEC 1945 FRANK POLICE DEPT TACOMA WASH.' Jay Gim explains that this arrest was an early battle in what became known as the Fish Wars. The video cuts back to Jay Gim, who introduces himself and welcomes viewers to 'Crash Course Native American History,' accompanied by upbeat intro music. The animation resumes, showing Billy Frank Jr. kneeling by the river, pulling a fishing net, as Jay Gim narrates that he was checking nets like his Nisqually Nation ancestors. A historical black-and-white photograph of 'Billy Frank Jr. fishing on the Nisqually River Washington (1973)' appears, showing him holding a large fish. Jay Gim continues, noting this was his first arrest but not his last, as he would be handcuffed over 50 more times. The video returns to Jay Gim, then to an animation of Billy Frank Jr. flanked by police officers, followed by a close-up of his face. The final animation depicts a diverse group of individuals, inspired by Billy Frank Jr.'s non-violent resistance, holding signs that read 'THIS LAND IS OUR LAND' and 'HONOR THE TREATIES,' with one person raising a fist in solidarity.

The video opens with a Man in a striped shirt, wearing glasses and a goatee, seated in a warmly lit room adorned with framed historical photographs and shelves of books and jars. He speaks directly to the camera, gesturing with a rolled paper, explaining that for Native people, food defines who they are. The scene transitions to a split-screen showing historical black-and-white photographs: on the left, Nisqually fisherman Johnny Bobb holding a large fish, and on the right, Nisqually Tribal Member Lou Ann Squally handling a fish in shallow water. The speaker notes the strong connection the Nisqually people had to fish, viewing salmon as gifts to be honored and protected, even if it meant facing jail time. This is immediately followed by a grainy historical image of arrests during the Fish Wars in Washington (1968–1970), depicting law enforcement officers detaining individuals. The video returns to the Man in the striped shirt, who continues speaking, introducing the concept of sovereignty. A split-screen graphic appears, defining 'Sovereignty' as 'The right of Native nations to govern themselves,' a definition he vocalizes. He then introduces 'Food Sovereignty,' defined on screen as 'The right of Native nations to use the foods that have sustained their communities since time immemorial,' also spoken aloud. The scene shifts to a close-up of hands holding a woven basket filled with colorful corn and herbs, representing traditional Indigenous foods, as the speaker elaborates on how Native communities had distinct, culturally defined relationships with their traditional, local, and seasonal foods before European arrival. This is followed by a display of dried corn cobs on a wooden surface. The video then shows Suquamish salmon preparation in Washington (2018), with salmon being smoked over a fire, before concluding with an illustrated calendar page for June, depicting strawberry plants, as the speaker mentions Algonquian tribes knew June as the 'strawberry moon.'

event_blocks.summary: The June calendar illustration, showing strawberry plants under a red sun, signals the start of berry season, as explained by the Man in a striped shirt. He then transitions to a September scene with pawpaw fruit, identifying it as the 'Pawpaw Moon' for the Shawnee, a time to enjoy the 'mango of the Midwest.' Speaking directly to the camera in a warmly lit room, the Man in striped shirt, wearing glasses and a goatee, discusses how Native peoples historically stockpiled food. This is illustrated by historical black-and-white photographs and a close-up video clip detailing the acorn processing methods of the Southwestern Pomo Indians of California (c. 1923), which involved drying, shelling, smashing, and flushing acorns to remove bitterness for use in various dishes, with baskets and hands actively engaged in grinding acorns into flour. The Man in striped shirt reappears, followed by an illustrated diagram of 'The Three Sisters'—corn, beans, and squash—growing synergistically on graph paper, a practice he attributes to nations like the Haudenosaunee and Cherokee, noting how these crops 'protected each other and grew better together.' The narrative then shifts to a map titled 'Native Land Dispossession,' contrasting pre-colonization Native land in orange with a 'Present Day' map showing a '99% Reduction' in orange areas, with land lost to the U.S. depicted in dark green. The Man in striped shirt concludes by stating that 'all that changed when Europeans began colonizing,' leading to Native nations losing 99% of their lands over 'just over 300 years,' all underscored by a gentle instrumental score.

Historical photograph of acorn storage.description: A black-and-white photograph of two individuals beside a large woven acorn storage basket in California (c. 1923), part of a sequence illustrating the traditional acorn processing methods of Native peoples, specifically the Southwestern Pomo Indians, who created flour from raw acorns.

Historical photograph of baskets used in.description: A black-and-white photograph showing baskets and acorns used in the preparation of acorn flour in California (c. 1923), part of a sequence illustrating the traditional acorn processing methods of Native peoples, specifically the Southwestern Pomo Indians, who created flour from raw acorns.

Man in striped shirt.description: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves, speaking directly to the camera in a clear, narrative voice, explaining traditional Native American practices, food cultivation, and the impact of colonization.

The Three Sisters illustration.description: An illustrated diagram on graph paper showing corn, beans, and squash growing together, labeled 'The Three Sisters,' explained by the narrator as crops that 'protected each other and grew better together,' as practiced by nations like the Haudenosaunee and Cherokee.

Acorn processing methods of the Southwes.description: A close-up video clip showing hands grinding acorns in a bowl, with text overlay, illustrating the traditional processing methods of the Southwestern Pomo Indians of California, who created flour from raw acorns by drying, shelling, smashing, and flushing out bitterness for use in soup, bread, or mush.

Native Land Dispossession map.description: A series of maps titled 'Native Land Dispossession,' showing pre-colonization Native land in orange transitioning to a 'Present Day' map with a '99% Reduction' in orange areas, visually representing the land lost to the U.S. due to European colonization over 'just over 300 years,' as narrated.

June calendar illustration.description: An illustrated calendar page for June, showing strawberry plants under a red sun, identified by the narrator as marking the beginning of berry season.

raw_segments: The video opens with an illustrated calendar page for June, depicting strawberry plants under a red sun, which the narrator, a Man in a striped shirt, identifies as marking the beginning of berry season. This transitions to a September scene with pawpaw fruit and trees, which he explains the Shawnee knew as the 'Pawpaw Moon,' a time to enjoy the 'mango of the Midwest.' The Man in striped shirt, wearing glasses and a goatee, then speaks directly to the camera in a warmly lit room, explaining how Native peoples stock-piled foods. Historical black-and-white photographs and a close-up video clip illustrate the traditional acorn processing methods of the Southwestern Pomo Indians of California (c. 1923), showing baskets and hands grinding acorns to create flour by drying, shelling, smashing, and flushing out bitterness for use in soup, bread, or mush. The Man in striped shirt reappears, followed by an illustrated diagram of 'The Three Sisters'—corn, beans, and squash—growing together on graph paper, which he describes as crops that 'protected each other and grew better together,' as practiced by nations like the Haudenosaunee and Cherokee. The video then presents a map titled 'Native Land Dispossession,' showing pre-colonization Native land in orange, transitioning to a 'Present Day' map with a '99% Reduction' in orange areas, highlighting land lost to the U.S. in dark green. The Man in striped shirt concludes, emphasizing that 'all that changed when Europeans began colonizing' and that Native nations lost 99% of their lands over 'just over 300 years,' all set to a gentle, underlying instrumental score.

event_blocks.summary: The US government's 19th and 20th-century policies forcibly removed Native peoples from their lands and utilized federal boarding schools to dismantle their cultures, separating children from families, languages, traditions, and foods, while punishing them for consuming what they grew on school farms, as evidenced by historical photographs of Carlisle Indian School students in the dining hall, kitchen, and garden. Simultaneously, settlers unsustainably exploited traditional food sources, exemplified by a hand-drawn animation of a Native figure hunting buffalo, which were essential for the Great Sioux's survival, and a historical photograph of a buffalo herd in Wyoming's Lamar Valley, contrasting with the 30 million buffalo that roamed the plains before the expansion of railroads led to their near extermination, depicted by a photograph of the completion of the first transcontinental railroad and a stark 1892 photograph of massive bison skulls, illustrating the deliberate elimination of this food source by 1887, with fewer than 100 buffalo remaining, a strategy reinforced by a 1867 quote from a U.S. Army soldier stating, 'Kill every buffalo you can. Every buffalo dead is an Indian gone,' and symbolized by Martin S. Garretson's 1883 illustration 'The End.' The narrative then shifts to the Fish Wars in Washington State, where Native Nations, identifying as 'salmon people,' signed treaties in the mid-19th century ceding millions of acres but retaining fishing rights, as highlighted by Article 3 of the 1854 Treaty with the Nisqually, Puyallup, etc., which secured 'The right of taking fish, at all usual and accustomed grounds and stations.' Despite these treaty protections, early 20th-century industrial salmon exploitation, shown through photographs of cannery workers at Apex Fish Co., fifty tons of salmon caught in Puget Sound, and commercial fishermen unloading their catch, led to salmon population collapse by the mid-20th century. This environmental degradation coincided with law enforcement crackdowns on Native fishers practicing their treaty rights, while commercial boats were less scrutinized, as depicted by images of law enforcement patrolling riverbanks and Native fishers with 'FISH FIGHT' signs. Billy Frank Jr., initially arrested for fishing as a matter of sustenance, became a central figure in the movement, alongside activists like Al Bridges and Marlon Brando, who supported Native fishers through fish-ins and demonstrations, leading to frequent arrests for Frank Jr., who stated, 'I was not a policy guy... I was a getting-arrested guy.' The struggle culminated in the 1974 landmark 'Boldt Decision,' which upheld treaty rights and granted Native nations half the harvestable fish catch, a significant victory for food sovereignty, which cannot be separated from rights.

Historical photograph of buffalo herd in.description: A black-and-white photograph showing a herd of buffalo grazing in a wide, open valley with hills in the background, captioned 'Buffalo herd in the Lamar Valley in Wyoming', framed with colorful geometric borders.

Historical photograph of the kitchen at.description: A black-and-white photo showing three women in aprons standing behind a long table filled with food, captioned 'The kitchen at Carlisle Indian School, Pennsylvania (c. 1895)', framed with colorful geometric borders.

Hand-drawn animation on lined paper.description: A colorful, hand-drawn illustration on lined paper depicting a Native figure on a blue horse hunting buffalo, with teepees, other figures in traditional attire, and animals in the background, rendered in a childlike, stylized manner.

Man in striped shirt.description: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork, a 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign, and shelves with books and candles. He speaks directly to the camera, gesturing expressively, and later crosses his arms, maintaining a serious demeanor, while a male narrator's voice discusses the Native American fishing rights movement, the Boldt Decision, and food sovereignty.

Historical photograph of Carlisle Indian.description: A black-and-white photo showing students tilling soil with tools in a garden, with a building and an adult supervisor in the background, captioned 'Carlisle Indian School students working in a garden, Pennsylvania (c. 1910)', framed with colorful geometric borders.

Historical photograph of students in the.description: A black-and-white photo showing numerous students in formal attire seated at long tables in a large dining hall, captioned 'Students in the dining hall at Carlisle Indian School, Pennsylvania (1901)', framed with colorful geometric borders.

Historical photograph of completion of t.description: A black-and-white photograph showing a large group of men gathered around two trains, with two men shaking hands at the center, captioned 'Completion of the first transcontinental railroad in the U.S., Utah (1869)', framed with colorful geometric borders.

Treaty with the Nisqually, Puyallup, etc.description: A close-up of the 1854 treaty text, with Article 3 highlighted in orange, stating 'The right of taking fish, at all usual and accustomed grounds and stations, is further secured to said Indians,' framed with colorful geometric borders.

Historical illustration 'The End' by Mar.description: A sepia-toned 1883 illustration depicting a desolate landscape littered with bison bones, a wagon, and a lone figure, symbolizing the end of the bison era, framed with colorful geometric borders.

Map of Nisqually territory.description: A green and yellow map of Washington State showing the Nisqually Reservation and the approximate ceded Nisqually territory, with labeled regions and a red triangle marking the reservation, framed with colorful geometric borders.

Historical photograph of bison skulls.description: A sepia-toned photograph from 1892 showing a massive pile of bison skulls in Michigan, with men standing at its base and one atop the mound, framed with colorful geometric borders.

Quote from U.S. Army soldier (1867).description: A text graphic displaying the quote: 'Kill every buffalo you can. Every buffalo dead is an Indian gone.' attributed to a U.S. Army soldier in 1867, set against a textured background with colorful borders.

Historical photograph of men with spruce.description: A sepia-toned photograph from 1905 showing men posing with a massive spruce log in the Cascade Mountains, Washington, with horses and a log cabin in the background, framed with colorful geometric borders.

Historical photograph of fifty tons of s.description: A black-and-white photograph from 1902 showing a massive pile of salmon, emphasizing the scale of commercial fishing in Puget Sound.

Native fishers practicing their treaty f.description: A colorized photograph from 1968–1970 showing a Native fisher in a boat with a 'FISH FIGHT' sign, casting a net, symbolizing resistance and the assertion of treaty rights.

Historical photograph of Billy Frank Jr..description: A black-and-white photo of Billy Frank Jr. (center) at a congressional hearing in Washington D.C., seated at a table with microphones, wearing a light-colored suit, surrounded by other attendees in formal attire.

Arrests during the Fish Wars photograph.description: A grainy, colorized historical image showing law enforcement officers arresting individuals during the Fish Wars in Washington (1968–1970), framed with colorful geometric borders, depicting tension and conflict over fishing rights.

Historical photograph of cannery workers.description: A black-and-white photograph from 1914 showing two women in aprons and hairnets standing beside a long table filled with rows of canned fish, illustrating industrial food processing.

Historical photograph of Muckleshoot pra.description: A black-and-white photograph from 1970 showing several men wading in the Green River, using long poles to fish, with cars and onlookers visible on the riverbank, illustrating treaty fishing rights in practice.

Historical photograph of commercial fish.description: A black-and-white image from 1906 showing two fishermen on a dock, pulling a large net filled with salmon, with a pile of fish already unloaded on the dock, emphasizing commercial fishing labor.

Historical photograph of law enforcement.description: A black-and-white photo showing police vehicles and officers gathering to arrest protestors in Washington (1970), with 'POLICE' written on a car, as the male narrator describes clashes with state police and violence faced by Native fishermen.

Historical photograph of Al Bridges.description: A black-and-white photo of activist Al Bridges speaking into a bullhorn at a fish-in in Washington (1970), wearing a headband and traditional attire, standing on a vehicle with trees in the background, as the male narrator mentions fish-ins where treaty rights were asserted.

Historical photograph of Marlon Brando a.description: A black-and-white photo of actor Marlon Brando (left) and Puyallup activist Bob Satiacum holding fish on the Puyallup River in Washington (1964), with Brando smiling, as the male narrator notes the movement attracted nationwide attention and support from celebrities like Brando.

Billy Frank Jr. quote graphic.description: A graphic displaying the quote: '[I] was not a policy guy... I was a getting-arrested guy.' attributed to Billy Frank Jr., shown alongside a portrait of him in a white shirt with a turquoise necklace, framed with colorful borders, as the male narrator quotes him and describes his frequent arrests.

The Boldt Decision graphic.description: A split-screen graphic with the Man in striped shirt on the left and a text box on the right defining 'The Boldt Decision' as the 1974 court ruling that upheld Treaty Rights and ruled that Native nations in Washington were entitled to half of the harvestable catch of fish, set against a dark textured background with colorful borders, while the male narrator explains this landmark decision.

raw_segments: The video opens with a Man in striped shirt, wearing glasses and a goatee, speaking directly to the camera in a warmly lit room adorned with framed historical photographs, Native art, and shelves, as somber, reflective music plays. He explains that between the 19th and 20th centuries, the US government forced Native people off their lands, using federal boarding schools to eradicate their cultures. As he speaks, the scene transitions to historical black-and-white photographs, first showing numerous students in formal attire seated at long tables in the Carlisle Indian School dining hall (1901), then three women in aprons standing behind a table laden with food in the school's kitchen (c. 1895), and finally students tilling soil with tools in a garden (c. 1910). The Man in striped shirt continues, detailing how Native children were separated from their families, languages, traditions, and foods, often fed low-quality rations and punished for eating anything they'd grown while laboring on school farms. The video returns to the speaker, now with a visible 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign on the wall behind him, as he shifts to discussing how settlers exploited traditional food sources unsustainably. A colorful hand-drawn animation on lined paper then depicts a Native figure on a blue horse hunting buffalo, with teepees and other figures in traditional attire in the background, illustrating his point that the Great Sioux, or 'Buffalo Nation,' viewed buffalo as essential for survival – for food, clothing, shelter, and even their creation story. This is followed by a historical photograph of a buffalo herd grazing in Wyoming's Lamar Valley. The Man in striped shirt then describes how 30 million buffalo roamed the plains in the early 19th century, but as railroads expanded west, hunters on those trains killed them, leading to a historical photograph of the completion of the first transcontinental railroad in Utah (1869), showing a large group of men gathered around two trains with a handshake at the center.

The segment opens with a stark 1892 historical photograph of a massive pile of bison skulls in Michigan, men standing at its base and one atop the mound, illustrating the scale of bison extermination. The Man in striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves, speaks directly to the camera, explaining that by 1887, fewer than 100 individual buffalo remained in the wild, a deliberate act that eliminated a traditional food source and destroyed the way of life for groups like the Great Sioux. A quote from a U.S. Army soldier from 1867 appears on screen, stating, 'Kill every buffalo you can. Every buffalo dead is an Indian gone,' reinforcing the speaker's point that the US government knew this would force Native Americans to sign treaties and cede land. This is followed by a sepia-toned 1883 illustration titled 'The End' by Martin S. Garretson, depicting a desolate landscape littered with bison bones, a wagon, and a lone figure, symbolizing the aftermath. The Man in striped shirt reappears, then a map of Washington State is shown, marking the Nisqually Reservation and the approximate ceded Nisqually territory, as the speaker transitions to discussing Billy Frank Jr. and the Fish Wars. He explains that in the mid-19th century, the Nisqually and other Native Nations, who identified as 'salmon people,' signed treaties with the US government, ceding millions of acres in Washington State, but under the big condition that they would keep the right to fish. A close-up of the 1854 Treaty with the Nisqually, Puyallup, etc. follows, with Article 3 highlighted: 'The right of taking fish, at all usual and accustomed grounds and stations, is further secured to said Indians,' underscoring these treaty-protected fishing rights. The segment concludes with a 1905 photograph of men posing with a massive spruce log in the Cascade Mountains, symbolizing resource exploitation, with the Man in striped shirt briefly reappearing to continue his narration about the decades of challenges.

The video opens with a series of historical black-and-white photographs illustrating early 20th-century salmon exploitation, beginning with cannery workers at Apex Fish Co. in 1914, two women in aprons and hairnets standing by a table of canned fish, emphasizing industrial processing. This transitions to a 1902 image of a massive pile of fifty tons of salmon caught in Puget Sound, showcasing the scale of commercial fishing, followed by a 1906 photograph of commercial fishermen laboriously unloading salmon from a net onto a dock. The Man in striped shirt's voice begins, explaining how logging, mining, commercial fishing, and other man-made activities led to the salmon population collapsing by the mid-20th century, much like the buffalo before them. The visuals then shift to the 1968–1970 period, with a colorized image of law enforcement patrolling riverbanks, followed by Native fishers practicing their treaty rights, including a close-up of a fisher in a boat with a 'FISH FIGHT' sign. The Man in striped shirt continues, detailing how state laws meant to protect fish led to law enforcement disproportionately cracking down on Native fishers on riverbanks, who took less than 5% of the annual catch, rather than offshore commercial boats. He then appears directly to the camera in a warmly lit room adorned with framed artwork, shelves, and a 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign, recounting how Billy Frank Jr. was first arrested for fishing, an act he initially saw as a matter of sustenance, not politics. Historical images of Billy Frank Jr. at a congressional hearing in Washington D.C. and Muckleshoot fishers in the Green River in 1970 are shown as the speaker explains that Frank Jr. and other Native fishers understood their treaty rights, knowing their exercise was legal under the U.S. government but illegal under state law, and crucial for their health, cultural identity, and survival, ultimately making their actions profoundly political. The segment concludes with the Man in striped shirt continuing his narration, linking these past struggles to present-day sovereignty and food rights.

The segment opens with a series of black-and-white historical photographs, as a male narrator's voice declares, "So the fish wars began." The first image shows Billy Frank Jr. and his family being confronted by law enforcement in 1970, with a man in a long coat pointing authoritatively. The narrator explains that by the 1960s, Frank Jr. and other Native activists were engaging in non-violent civil disobedience, staging demonstrations like fish-ins. This is visually supported by a photo of activist Al Bridges speaking into a bullhorn at a fish-in. The narrator continues, detailing the sabotage, violence, and clashes with state police faced by Native fishermen, coinciding with a photo of law enforcement vehicles and officers gathering to arrest protestors. The movement gained nationwide attention, drawing support from celebrities like Marlon Brando, who is seen in a photo with Puyallup activist Bob Satiacum fishing on the Puyallup River in 1964. The narrator then highlights Billy Frank Jr.'s frequent arrests, quoting him: "'I was not a policy guy... I was a getting-arrested guy.'" The video transitions to a modern-day interview with the Man in striped shirt, who speaks directly to the camera in a warmly lit room adorned with artwork and a 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign. A graphic displaying Billy Frank Jr.'s quote appears. The narrator explains that in 1974, Frank Jr. and other activists finally had their day in court, leading to the landmark 'Boldt Decision.' A graphic explaining 'The Boldt Decision' appears beside the Man in striped shirt, detailing the 1974 ruling that upheld treaty rights and granted Native nations half the harvestable fish catch. An air horn sound effect punctuates the narrator's excitement for this "huge win." The Man in striped shirt continues speaking, crossing his arms with a serious demeanor, as the narrator concludes by emphasizing that food sovereignty cannot be separated from rights.

event_blocks.summary: A man in a striped shirt, speaking from a warmly lit room featuring a 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign, explains the profound impact of disruptions to traditional food systems on Native communities, noting that every Native nation in the U.S. shares a common narrative of separation and resilience. He introduces frybread, a 'delectable, deep-fried disc of flour, salt, water, and baking powder' and a 'staple at powwows,' as a complex example. Historical black-and-white photographs of Navajo people on the Bosque Redondo Indian Reservation and a map detailing their 'nearly 500-kilometer journey' to New Mexico illustrate frybread's origins in the 1860s, when forced displacement and unsuitable land led to government rations of white flour, a period during which 'hundreds died.' Modern footage from Wyoming PBS in 2019 shows the ingredients for frybread—flour, nonfat dry milk, salt, and baking powder—being mixed, connecting this historical context of survival and adaptation to contemporary cultural practice. While some view frybread as a symbol of torn food systems contributing to health issues like diabetes, others see it as a symbol of comfort and resilience, 'making something from nothing to feed everybody.' This same resilience is evident in numerous Native food initiatives across the U.S., such as the Muckleshoot Food Sovereignty Project in Washington, which hosts harvest festivals and teaches traditional food preparation, exemplified by visuals of smoked salmon from Cherokee traditional salmon smoking in Washington (2023). The Cherokee Nation Heirloom Seed Program in Oklahoma (2016) distributes free heirloom seed packets to encourage ancestral crop cultivation, and the Three Sisters Project at Iowa State University Iowa partners with native gardeners to study traditional agricultural practices, shown with corn, beans, and squash growing together.

Historical photograph of Corn Fields - B.description: A black-and-white photograph depicting Navajo people working in corn fields on the Bosque Redondo Indian Reservation in New Mexico (c. 1863–1868), framed with colorful geometric borders. This image illustrates the challenges faced by survivors who found the land 'wasn't good for growing their traditional staples of corn, beans, and squash.'

Wyoming PBS footage of making frybread.description: A modern kitchen scene showing ingredients for frybread (flour, nonfat dry milk, salt, baking powder) on a wooden counter, with a woman mixing them in a blue bowl. Text overlay reads 'Making frybread on the Wind River Indian Reservation Wyoming (2019)' and the Wyoming PBS logo is visible.

Map of Navajo Homelands and Bosque Redon.description: A green-toned map showing the Navajo Homelands in orange, with a red wavy line indicating the forced relocation to the Bosque Redondo Reservation and Internment Camp in New Mexico, framed with colorful geometric borders. This map visually represents the 'nearly 500-kilometer journey' mentioned by the speaker.

Historical photograph of Navajo on the B.description: A black-and-white photograph showing Navajo people gathered around horses and buildings on the Bosque Redondo Indian Reservation in New Mexico (c. 1863–1868), framed with colorful geometric borders. This image illustrates the forced relocation of the Navajo Nation, a journey where 'hundreds died' to a reservation in what's now New Mexico.

Frybread.description: Golden-brown, puffy fried bread shown being lifted from a hot oil pan, with a close-up of its crispy texture and golden surface. The man speaking explains it was made from flour, sugar, and lard due to rations, and is seen by some as a reminder of broken traditional food systems leading to health issues, while others view it as a symbol of comfort and resilience.

Man in striped shirt.description: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a room with framed artwork and shelves in the background, speaking directly to the camera about the history and cultural significance of frybread, native food initiatives, and specific tribal projects like the Muckleshoot Food Sovereignty Project, Cherokee Nation Heirloom Seed Program, and the Three Sisters Project. The background now includes a visible 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign.

Three Sisters Project at Iowa State Univ.description: A woman in a blue jacket and black pants standing in a field observing rows of corn, beans, and squash growing together, with close-ups of the plants in the soil. The speaker describes this as the Three Sisters Project at Iowa State University, which partners with native gardeners to study how growing corn, beans, and squash together works.

Cherokee Nation Heirloom Seed Program Ok.description: Clear plastic bags labeled 'Cherokee White Eagle Corn' and other heirloom seeds, with the Cherokee Nation Administration Support Dept. address printed on the bags. The speaker explains that the Cherokee Nation shares these free heirloom seed packets with their members, encouraging them to grow and eat the same crops as their ancestors.

Frybread / Navajo Taco stand.description: A small white and blue food stand with a large 'NAVAJO TACO' sign, offering 'HOT FRESH FRY BREAD $5.00', with a customer placing an order at the window.

Cherokee traditional salmon smoking Wash.description: Smoked salmon strips hanging in a smokehouse, with a text overlay indicating the location and year. This visual is linked to the Muckleshoot Food Sovereignty Project's efforts to teach tribal members how to prepare salmon.

Muckleshoot huckleberry harvest Washingt.description: A person in a red shirt and woven hat harvesting huckleberries in a forest, with a close-up of the dark purple and red berries in a woven basket. This visual is contextualized by the speaker mentioning the Muckleshoot Food Sovereignty Project, which hosts harvest festivals, maintains community gardens, and teaches tribal members how to prepare salmon and elk.

raw_segments: Against a backdrop of warm lighting, framed artwork, and a 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign, a Man in a striped shirt speaks directly to the camera, gesturing with a rolled paper, explaining how disruptions to traditional food systems, like those experienced by the Great Sioux and Nisqually, impact the health and well-being of Native communities. He emphasizes that every Native nation in the US has faced such disruptions, leading to a common story of separation and resilience, underscored by a subtle, reflective instrumental track. He then introduces frybread as an example, describing it as a 'delectable, deep-fried disc of flour, salt, water, and baking powder' and a 'staple at powwows,' as visuals of golden-brown frybread appear, first plain, then topped with lettuce, tomatoes, and cheese. The Man details frybread's 'complicated past,' tracing its origins to the 1860s when the US government forced thousands of Navajo Nation members from their homelands. Historical black-and-white photographs of Navajo people on the Bosque Redondo Indian Reservation and a map illustrating the 'nearly 500-kilometer journey' to the reservation in New Mexico appear, as he recounts how 'hundreds died' and survivors found the land unsuitable for traditional crops like 'corn, beans, and squash,' leading to government rations of white flour. The segment concludes with modern footage from Wyoming (2019) showing ingredients for frybread—flour, nonfat dry milk, salt, and baking powder—being mixed in a kitchen, visually connecting the historical context of survival and adaptation to the contemporary cultural practice of making frybread.

The segment opens with a close-up of hands mixing ingredients in a blue bowl, part of Wyoming PBS footage showing frybread being made on the Wind River Indian Reservation in 2019, followed by a shot of golden-brown frybread being lifted from a hot oil pan. A man in a striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with a 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign, speaks directly to the camera, explaining that frybread, made from flour, sugar, and lard, became a staple for Native Nations given similar rations. He notes that while some today see frybread as a reminder of traditional food systems being torn apart, contributing to high rates of diabetes and heart disease, others view it as a symbol of comfort and resilience, making something from nothing to feed everybody. Gentle, uplifting music plays in the background as the man continues, stating that this same resilience, which ignited the 'fish wars' and brought frybread, is evident in numerous native food initiatives across the U.S. The scene transitions to still images: a 'Navajo Taco' stand, a crowded 'Dine Cafe', and a person harvesting huckleberries in Washington in 2022, with a close-up of the berries in a woven basket. The man's narration highlights the Muckleshoot Food Sovereignty Project, which hosts harvest festivals, maintains community gardens, and teaches tribal members to prepare salmon and elk, coinciding with visuals of smoked salmon hanging in a smokehouse from Cherokee traditional salmon smoking in Washington (2023). He then mentions the Cherokee Nation sharing free heirloom seed packets with members to encourage growing ancestral crops, shown as bags of seeds from the Cherokee Nation Heirloom Seed Program in Oklahoma (2016). The segment concludes with a woman in a blue jacket in a field at the 'Three Sisters Project at Iowa State University Iowa', with close-ups of corn, beans, and squash growing together, as the man explains this project partners with native gardeners to study these traditional agricultural practices.

event_blocks.summary: Shawn Sherman, 'The Sioux Chef,' is introduced as Native American chefs, including those preparing Indigenous cuisine for the White House Tribal Nations Summit in Washington, D.C. (2023), are highlighted for their growing influence through restaurants, cookbooks, and the promotion of traditional foods. A segment titled 'Indigikitchen with Mariah Gladstone' demonstrates the preparation of pemmican, involving blending dried meat into powder, incorporating wild rice, forming patties, and frying. Mariah Gladstone, a member of the Blackfeet and Cherokee Nations and host of Indigikitchen, shares traditional food knowledge, teaching pemmican and balsamic manoomin with dandelion greens. A man in a striped shirt, with glasses, a goatee, and ear piercings, explains that colonization violently disconnected Native Nations from their ancestral food systems, which for millennia sustained their communities through hunting, fishing, growing, and gathering. The video then showcases a buffet of traditional Indigenous foods in Alaska (2016), featuring dried fish and a meal of soup with greens, followed by Suquamish salmon preparation in Washington (2018), where salmon is smoked over a fire. The narrative emphasizes the understanding among Native people that community nourishment sustains existence, driving many Native Nations to protect traditional foods. The summary concludes with an aerial view of a dam on the Nisqually River, noting the ongoing threats to salmon populations.

Traditional Indigenous foods Alaska (201.description: A buffet scene with hands serving food, including a plate with a bowl of soup and a side of greens, and a large tray of dried fish, set in a rustic wooden interior.

Native American chefs preparing Indigeno.description: A group of chefs in white uniforms, including one wearing a face mask, standing behind a food preparation counter with ingredients and tools, alongside a woman in a blazer, in a modern kitchen setting.

Shawn Sherman, 'The Sioux Chef'.description: A close-up of a person's ear and hair, with on-screen text identifying them as Shawn Sherman, 'The Sioux Chef,' indicating their role as a prominent Indigenous culinary figure.

Man in striped shirt.description: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves, speaking directly to the camera, serving as the primary narrator for this segment, discussing Native chefs' impact, Mariah Gladstone's work, the historical context of Indigenous food systems, the effects of colonization, and the current challenges faced by the Nisqually River.

Dam on the Nisqually River.description: An aerial view of a large concrete dam spanning the Nisqually River, surrounded by green trees and a road, which the narrator explicitly states still poses threats to the salmon, highlighting its ongoing environmental and cultural impact on Indigenous communities.

Suquamish salmon preparation Washington.description: Salmon being smoked over a fire using a traditional wooden rack, with flames visible beneath the fish, emphasizing traditional food preparation methods.

Indigikitchen with Mariah Gladstone.description: A cooking demonstration showing the steps to make pemmican: blending super dry meat into powder, adding wild rice, pressing into patties, and frying, with on-screen text guiding each step. This demonstration is hosted by Mariah Gladstone, identified by the narrator as a member of the Blackfeet and Cherokee Nations and the host of Indigikitchen, an online cooking show, who shares traditional food knowledge, teaching viewers how to make pemmican and balsamic manoomin with dandelion greens.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a person's ear and hair, identified by on-screen text as Shawn Sherman, 'The Sioux Chef,' set against a backdrop of background music. The scene transitions to a group of Native American chefs in white chef uniforms preparing Indigenous cuisine for the White House Tribal Nations Summit in Washington, D.C. (2023), with a woman in a blazer standing beside them, as the narration highlights how Native chefs are making their mark in the culinary world by opening restaurants, publishing cookbooks, and bringing traditional foods into the limelight. This leads into a cooking demonstration titled 'Indigikitchen with Mariah Gladstone,' showing the preparation of pemmican: blending super dry meat into powder, adding wild rice, pressing into patties, and frying. The narrator identifies Mariah Gladstone as a member of the Blackfeet and Cherokee Nations and the host of Indigikitchen, an online cooking show, who shares traditional food knowledge, teaching viewers how to make pemmican and balsamic manoomin with dandelion greens. A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, appears seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves, speaking directly to the camera, explaining that for thousands of years, Native Nations hunted, fished, grew, and gathered food that sustained their communities, but under colonization, they faced violent disconnection from those food systems. The video then cuts to a buffet of traditional Indigenous foods in Alaska (2016), including a display of dried fish and a plate with a bowl of soup and a side of greens, followed by a scene of Suquamish salmon preparation in Washington (2018), with salmon being smoked over a fire. The narrator continues, emphasizing that Native people recognize that nourishing a community supports its ability to exist, leading many Native Nations to fight to keep traditional foods in their communities. The segment concludes with an aerial view of a dam on the Nisqually River, as the narrator states that the salmon in the Nisqually River still face threats.

event_blocks.summary: The speaker details how dams, habitat destruction, and overharvesting have impacted resources, but notes that the State of Washington now engages in co-management of the river and fish in partnership with the Nisqually and other Native Nations. Billy Frank Jr.'s persistent activism in safeguarding tribal treaty rights is emphasized, continuing until his passing in 2014, after which he received a posthumous Presidential Medal of Freedom and a national wildlife refuge was named in his honor. The upcoming episode will explore Native Americans' initial encounters with Europeans. The speaker expresses gratitude to the audience for viewing this installment of Crash Course Native American History, filmed in their Indianapolis, Indiana studio with assistance from numerous individuals. Viewers are encouraged to support the mission of keeping Crash Course accessible by joining the Patreon community, as upbeat, slightly melancholic background music swells, becoming more pronounced as the episode concludes.
raw_segments: A male speaker explains that dams, habitat destruction, and overharvesting are issues, but the state of Washington now co-manages the river and fish alongside the Nisqually and other Native Nations. He then highlights Billy Frank Jr.'s continued activism in protecting tribal treaty rights until his death in 2014, noting that a year later he was posthumously awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom and had a national wildlife refuge named in his honor. The speaker then announces that the next episode will delve into Native Americans' first contact with Europeans, before thanking viewers for watching this episode of Crash Course Native American History, which was filmed at their studio in Indianapolis, Indiana, and made with the help of many people. He concludes by inviting viewers to join their community on Patreon to support keeping Crash Course free, all while upbeat, slightly melancholic background music plays, becoming more prominent towards the end.

global_entity_library.Figure in red coat: A hand-drawn character with dark hair, wearing a red coat, identified as Billy Frank Jr. The audio reveals he was 14 years old in 1945 when he was arrested on the Nisqually River for checking fishing nets, shouting 'LEAVE ME ALONE! I LIVE!' He is depicted surrendering, being escorted by officers, in a mugshot, fishing, and later inspiring non-violent resistance. The audio notes he was a member of the Nisqually Nation and would be arrested over 50 more times.
global_entity_library.Man in striped shirt: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves, speaking directly to the camera, serving as the primary narrator for this segment, discussing Native chefs' impact, Mariah Gladstone's work, the historical context of Indigenous food systems, the effects of colonization, and the current challenges faced by the Nisqually River.
A black-and-white photo of Billy Frank Jr. (center) at a congressional hearing in Washington D.C., seated at a table with microphones, wearing a light-colored suit, surrounded by other attendees in formal attire.
global_entity_library.Police officers: Silhouetted and later blue-uniformed figures who arrest Billy Frank Jr. in 1945, with one officer heard saying, 'You're under arrest!' They are depicted escorting him and later flanking him in subsequent arrests.
global_entity_library.Traditional Indigenous foods basket: A close-up of hands holding a woven basket filled with colorful corn, herbs, and other traditional Indigenous foods, with a turquoise beaded necklace visible on the person’s chest, shown as the speaker describes the distinct, culturally defined relationships Native communities had with their traditional foods.
global_entity_library.Arrests during the Fish Wars photograph: A grainy, colorized historical image showing law enforcement officers arresting individuals during the Fish Wars in Washington (1968–1970), framed with colorful geometric borders, depicting tension and conflict over fishing rights.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of Johnny Bobb: A black-and-white photo of a man holding a large fish, captioned 'Nisqually fisherman Johnny Bobb', framed with colorful geometric borders, shown as the speaker discusses the Nisqually people's connection to fish.
global_entity_library.Suquamish salmon preparation footage: A video clip showing salmon being smoked over a fire using a traditional wooden rack, captioned 'Suquamish salmon preparation Washington (2018)', framed with colorful geometric borders, shown as the speaker describes the distinct, culturally defined relationships Native communities had with their traditional foods.
global_entity_library.Sovereignty definition graphic: A split-screen graphic with the Man in striped shirt on the left and a text box on the right defining 'Sovereignty' as 'The right of Native nations to govern themselves', set against a green textured background with colorful borders, with the definition being spoken aloud by the Man in striped shirt.
global_entity_library.Food Sovereignty definition graphic: A split-screen graphic with the Man in striped shirt on the left and a text box on the right defining 'Food Sovereignty' as 'The right of Native nations to use the foods that have sustained their communities since time immemorial', set against a green textured background with colorful borders, with the definition being spoken aloud by the Man in striped shirt.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of Lou Ann Squally: A black-and-white photo of a woman crouching in shallow water handling a fish, captioned 'Nisqually Tribal Member Lou Ann Squally', framed with colorful geometric borders, shown as the speaker discusses the Nisqually people's connection to fish.
global_entity_library.Dried corn cobs display: A wooden surface displaying rows of dried corn cobs with multicolored kernels, alongside jars of beans and other grains, shown as the speaker describes the distinct, culturally defined relationships Native communities had with their traditional foods.
global_entity_library.June calendar illustration: An illustrated calendar page for June, showing strawberry plants under a red sun, identified by the narrator as marking the beginning of berry season.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of acorn storage: A black-and-white photograph of two individuals beside a large woven acorn storage basket in California (c. 1923), part of a sequence illustrating the traditional acorn processing methods of Native peoples, specifically the Southwestern Pomo Indians, who created flour from raw acorns.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of baskets used in: A black-and-white photograph showing baskets and acorns used in the preparation of acorn flour in California (c. 1923), part of a sequence illustrating the traditional acorn processing methods of Native peoples, specifically the Southwestern Pomo Indians, who created flour from raw acorns.
global_entity_library.The Three Sisters illustration: An illustrated diagram on graph paper showing corn, beans, and squash growing together, labeled 'The Three Sisters,' explained by the narrator as crops that 'protected each other and grew better together,' as practiced by nations like the Haudenosaunee and Cherokee.
global_entity_library.Acorn processing methods of the Southwes: A close-up video clip showing hands grinding acorns in a bowl, with text overlay, illustrating the traditional processing methods of the Southwestern Pomo Indians of California, who created flour from raw acorns by drying, shelling, smashing, and flushing out bitterness for use in soup, bread, or mush.
global_entity_library.Native Land Dispossession map: A series of maps titled 'Native Land Dispossession,' showing pre-colonization Native land in orange transitioning to a 'Present Day' map with a '99% Reduction' in orange areas, visually representing the land lost to the U.S. due to European colonization over 'just over 300 years,' as narrated.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of buffalo herd in: A black-and-white photograph showing a herd of buffalo grazing in a wide, open valley with hills in the background, captioned 'Buffalo herd in the Lamar Valley in Wyoming', framed with colorful geometric borders.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of the kitchen at: A black-and-white photo showing three women in aprons standing behind a long table filled with food, captioned 'The kitchen at Carlisle Indian School, Pennsylvania (c. 1895)', framed with colorful geometric borders.
global_entity_library.Hand-drawn animation on lined paper: A colorful, hand-drawn illustration on lined paper depicting a Native figure on a blue horse hunting buffalo, with teepees, other figures in traditional attire, and animals in the background, rendered in a childlike, stylized manner.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of Carlisle Indian: A black-and-white photo showing students tilling soil with tools in a garden, with a building and an adult supervisor in the background, captioned 'Carlisle Indian School students working in a garden, Pennsylvania (c. 1910)', framed with colorful geometric borders.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of students in the: A black-and-white photo showing numerous students in formal attire seated at long tables in a large dining hall, captioned 'Students in the dining hall at Carlisle Indian School, Pennsylvania (1901)', framed with colorful geometric borders.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of completion of t: A black-and-white photograph showing a large group of men gathered around two trains, with two men shaking hands at the center, captioned 'Completion of the first transcontinental railroad in the U.S., Utah (1869)', framed with colorful geometric borders.
global_entity_library.Treaty with the Nisqually, Puyallup, etc: A close-up of the 1854 treaty text, with Article 3 highlighted in orange, stating 'The right of taking fish, at all usual and accustomed grounds and stations, is further secured to said Indians,' framed with colorful geometric borders.
global_entity_library.Historical illustration 'The End' by Mar: A sepia-toned 1883 illustration depicting a desolate landscape littered with bison bones, a wagon, and a lone figure, symbolizing the end of the bison era, framed with colorful geometric borders.
global_entity_library.Map of Nisqually territory: A green and yellow map of Washington State showing the Nisqually Reservation and the approximate ceded Nisqually territory, with labeled regions and a red triangle marking the reservation, framed with colorful geometric borders.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of bison skulls: A sepia-toned photograph from 1892 showing a massive pile of bison skulls in Michigan, with men standing at its base and one atop the mound, framed with colorful geometric borders.
global_entity_library.Quote from U.S. Army soldier (1867): A text graphic displaying the quote: 'Kill every buffalo you can. Every buffalo dead is an Indian gone.' attributed to a U.S. Army soldier in 1867, set against a textured background with colorful borders.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of men with spruce: A sepia-toned photograph from 1905 showing men posing with a massive spruce log in the Cascade Mountains, Washington, with horses and a log cabin in the background, framed with colorful geometric borders.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of fifty tons of s: A black-and-white photograph from 1902 showing a massive pile of salmon, emphasizing the scale of commercial fishing in Puget Sound.
global_entity_library.Native fishers practicing their treaty f: A colorized photograph from 1968–1970 showing a Native fisher in a boat with a 'FISH FIGHT' sign, casting a net, symbolizing resistance and the assertion of treaty rights.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of cannery workers: A black-and-white photograph from 1914 showing two women in aprons and hairnets standing beside a long table filled with rows of canned fish, illustrating industrial food processing.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of Muckleshoot pra: A black-and-white photograph from 1970 showing several men wading in the Green River, using long poles to fish, with cars and onlookers visible on the riverbank, illustrating treaty fishing rights in practice.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of commercial fish: A black-and-white image from 1906 showing two fishermen on a dock, pulling a large net filled with salmon, with a pile of fish already unloaded on the dock, emphasizing commercial fishing labor.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of law enforcement: A black-and-white photo showing police vehicles and officers gathering to arrest protestors in Washington (1970), with 'POLICE' written on a car, as the male narrator describes clashes with state police and violence faced by Native fishermen.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of Al Bridges: A black-and-white photo of activist Al Bridges speaking into a bullhorn at a fish-in in Washington (1970), wearing a headband and traditional attire, standing on a vehicle with trees in the background, as the male narrator mentions fish-ins where treaty rights were asserted.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of Marlon Brando a: A black-and-white photo of actor Marlon Brando (left) and Puyallup activist Bob Satiacum holding fish on the Puyallup River in Washington (1964), with Brando smiling, as the male narrator notes the movement attracted nationwide attention and support from celebrities like Brando.
global_entity_library.Billy Frank Jr. quote graphic: A graphic displaying the quote: '[I] was not a policy guy... I was a getting-arrested guy.' attributed to Billy Frank Jr., shown alongside a portrait of him in a white shirt with a turquoise necklace, framed with colorful borders, as the male narrator quotes him and describes his frequent arrests.
global_entity_library.The Boldt Decision graphic: A split-screen graphic with the Man in striped shirt on the left and a text box on the right defining 'The Boldt Decision' as the 1974 court ruling that upheld Treaty Rights and ruled that Native nations in Washington were entitled to half of the harvestable catch of fish, set against a dark textured background with colorful borders, while the male narrator explains this landmark decision.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of Corn Fields - B: A black-and-white photograph depicting Navajo people working in corn fields on the Bosque Redondo Indian Reservation in New Mexico (c. 1863–1868), framed with colorful geometric borders. This image illustrates the challenges faced by survivors who found the land 'wasn't good for growing their traditional staples of corn, beans, and squash.'
global_entity_library.Wyoming PBS footage of making frybread: A modern kitchen scene showing ingredients for frybread (flour, nonfat dry milk, salt, baking powder) on a wooden counter, with a woman mixing them in a blue bowl. Text overlay reads 'Making frybread on the Wind River Indian Reservation Wyoming (2019)' and the Wyoming PBS logo is visible.
global_entity_library.Map of Navajo Homelands and Bosque Redon: A green-toned map showing the Navajo Homelands in orange, with a red wavy line indicating the forced relocation to the Bosque Redondo Reservation and Internment Camp in New Mexico, framed with colorful geometric borders. This map visually represents the 'nearly 500-kilometer journey' mentioned by the speaker.
global_entity_library.Historical photograph of Navajo on the B: A black-and-white photograph showing Navajo people gathered around horses and buildings on the Bosque Redondo Indian Reservation in New Mexico (c. 1863–1868), framed with colorful geometric borders. This image illustrates the forced relocation of the Navajo Nation, a journey where 'hundreds died' to a reservation in what's now New Mexico.
